key_strengths: Widest moat in global banking: #1 in US deposits, credit cards, and investment banking revenue with 20% ROTCE vs. 12-14% peers
Fortress balance sheet with CET1 of 14.5% (300 bps above requirement) and $57B pre-provision earnings absorbing severe credit losses
Exceptional fee diversification across consumer banking, investment banking, trading, and asset management creates natural earnings hedge
Best-in-class efficiency ratio of 52-53% driven by $20B annual technology investment and unmatched scale
21-year track record under Dimon compounding tangible book value at ~10% annually through multiple crises
key_risks: Valuation at all-time-high P/TBV of 2.66x (10-year median 1.57x) prices in perfection with no margin of safety
CEO succession imminent within 2-3 years with likely 10-15% multiple compression during transition
ROTCE normalization from 20% toward management's 17% guide; recession could compress to 12-14%
Negative margin of safety: price exceeds base IV by 10% with asymmetric downside (32% down vs. 15% up)
Regulatory uncertainty from Basel III endgame rules could increase capital requirements and constrain buybacks
red_flags: Dimon sold 11%+ of holdings in early 2025 ($233M in February alone) — pace of insider selling has accelerated
Apple Card portfolio acquisition introduces $2.2B in unseasoned consumer credit risk with uncertain loss curves
2025 net income declined slightly from 2024 peak ($57.0B vs. $58.5B), suggesting cyclical earnings peak may have passed
Quant DCF model completely unreliable for bank valuation — IV outputs ($142 base) are meaningless for banks
buy_triggers: Stock pulls back to 1.5-2.0x TBVPS ($160-$215) providing genuine margin of safety
Dimon succession completed smoothly with new CEO demonstrating continuity of fortress philosophy
Recession-driven banking sell-off compresses JPM to unjustified levels relative to earnings power
sell_triggers: P/TBV exceeds 3.0x (extreme overvaluation disconnected from fundamentals)
ROTCE falls below 12% for two consecutive quarters signaling structural deterioration
New CEO pursues large transformative acquisition at excessive price breaking capital allocation discipline
valuation_summary: At $286.56, JPM trades at 2.66x tangible book value ($107.56 TBVPS) — the all-time high of its historical P/TBV range (10-year median 1.57x). Bank-appropriate valuation methods (P/TBV, normalized earnings power, residual income) yield a base IV of ~$260, meaning the stock trades at a ~10% premium to fair value. Forward P/E of 12.2x on consensus $23.49 EPS is reasonable but fully prices in above-trend earnings. The DCF model is unreliable for banks. This is a world-class franchise fairly valued to modestly overvalued.
compact_checklist: JPM collects the spread between $1T+ in cheap deposits and higher-rate loans, plus fees on every capital markets, payments, and wealth management transaction across 82M+ households and global institutional clients — customers stay because switching treasury and custody integrations takes months and carries operational risk.

A $20B/year technology budget, #1 positions in US deposits, credit cards, and investment banking, and 20% ROTCE vs. 12-14% for peers create a scale and switching-cost advantage that no competitor or fintech can replicate across all three segments simultaneously.

20% ROTCE in FY2025 is roughly double the 10-12% cost of equity, the efficiency ratio of 52-53% is best-in-class among universal banks, and tangible book value per share has compounded at ~10% annually since 2006.

Dimon's 21-year tenure produced ~10% annual book value compounding, a perfectly timed First Republic acquisition, and $34.6B in buybacks in FY2025 alone — but he sold 11%+ of his shares in early 2025 and his exit within 2-3 years is the biggest open question.

CET1 of 14.5% sits 300 bps above the regulatory minimum, the Fed just cut JPM's stress capital buffer to 2.5%, and $57B in pre-provision earnings can absorb a doubling of credit losses before capital is even threatened.

At 2.66x tangible book (vs. 10-year median of 1.57x), the stock requires JPM to sustain 18-20% ROTCE indefinitely, execute a flawless CEO succession, and avoid any meaningful credit cycle — all simultaneously.
There is none — the price exceeds our base IV of $260 by 10%, with 32% downside to the bear case ($195) versus only 15% upside to the bull case ($330).

ROTCE falling below 12% for two consecutive quarters, a new CEO making a large dilutive acquisition, or Basel III endgame rules materially increasing capital requirements would each break the investment case.
source_summary: JPM Q4 2025 earnings release and presentation, SEC filings, JPM 2025 Investor Day, Yahoo Finance (yfinance auto-fetch), FDIC deposit data, Dealogic league tables, GuruFocus TBVPS data, MacroTrends historical P/B, Federal Reserve DFAST 2025 results, CNBC/Bloomberg reporting, Seeking Alpha, Investing.com
change_notes: Initial analysis. JPM is a world-class banking franchise with the widest moat in global banking, but at 2.66x TBVPS (all-time high) with negative margin of safety, imminent CEO succession, and ROTCE normalization ahead, the stock prices in perfection. Watch verdict — wait for 1.5-2.0x TBVPS entry ($160-$215).
